top_strength: No-hidden-fees pitch — Danah explicitly listed every fee Einstein doesn't charge (credit card, transportation, elevator, assembly) and named the No Worries Damage Coverage with the $2,000 figure, giving the customer a clear, confident picture of what they're paying for.
coaching_note: Save Your Ass is the biggest missed opportunity (0/10) and the highest-leverage fix — Danah skipped the prep speech entirely. For a 2–2.5 hour minimum-range move, even shaving 15 minutes through consolidation coaching protects the customer and the company. She should also open every call with the agenda statement and ask "How are you today?" — the call jumped straight to pricing with no warm-up, which cost her 5 of 7 points in S1 before she said anything substantive.
phone_presence_notes: Delivery reads as scripted throughout — no affirmations, no empathy markers, no rapport moments, and a mispronunciation of the customer's name ("Audem" for Autumn) that went uncorrected; customer's responses were almost entirely monosyllabic by the end.

top_strength: The no-hidden-fees and pricing walkthrough were thorough and accurate — she listed every fee exclusion (credit card, transportation, stair, assembly, service fees), explained the $96/hr all-in rate clearly, and delivered the No Worries Damage Coverage with the correct $2,000 figure and "repair, replace, or reimburse" language.
coaching_note: Save Your Ass was completely absent — 0/10 on the single highest-leverage section. Before closing the booking, Danah should deliver the prep speech: "A little prep before move day makes a big difference on your bill — consolidate loose items like kids' toys, drawer contents, and photos off walls. Anything small enough, throw it in your car. Shorter load time means a shorter clock." That one speech recovers 10 points and directly benefits the customer's wallet and move-day efficiency.

top_strength: Therese caught the TV disclosure at the right moment — after confirming the booking, she asked "Is there anything else you'd like to add to the move?" and Melinda revealed two TVs. Therese handled it immediately with specific knowledge (box available for up to 42", blanket wrap for the 55"), confirmed with the customer, and looped it into the confirmation recap. That's exactly the kind of detail catch that prevents day-of surprises.
coaching_note: Save Your Ass was entirely absent — 0/10 on the highest-value section. Therese knew this was a partial move and that Melinda already planned to self-haul smaller items with her sister and car. That's a perfect setup for the prep speech: "Since you're already planning to take a carload, the more you consolidate before we arrive — drawer items, small boxes, things off the walls — the faster we move, and the lower your bill." The customer even said "I can do some of the stuff myself" — that's the exact opening. Missing Save Your Ass on a move where the customer is already self-helping is a direct lost opportunity to protect the customer's bill and the company's efficiency on move day.

This is a high-friction surprise for long-distance customers and she neutralized it before it became a complaint.
coaching_note: Save Your Ass and Clock were both 0 — that's 14 points left on the table and the primary reason this call fails. For a full 4BR long-distance move where the customer is already self-packing, the prep speech is directly applicable: "The more consolidated your items are before we arrive, the faster we move and the lower your bill." Coach Therese to deliver all four beats (prep = lower bill, consolidation examples, take a carload, "does that make sense?") before confirming the booking. Clock is a separate scripted block that should always run — minimum, prorating, start time — regardless of move complexity.
phone_presence_notes: Polite and uses "wonderful" and "great" as affirmations, but when the customer expressed sticker shock ("wow, yeah I kind of expected it to be expensive"), Therese responded flatly with "yes it is a big move" — missing the empathy cue and any value reframe; warmth is present but not responsive.
